analogy: Think of "Secularism" through the chapter's own opening images: SecularismSecularismPolitical Theory 111 Chapter 8 Secularism When different cultures and communities exist within the same country, how should a democratic state ensure equality for each of them? This is the question that emerged in the previous chapter. In this chapter we will try and see... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 8.1 WHAT IS SECULARISM?, 8.2 SECULAR STATE, LET’S DO IT D o, 8.3 THE WESTERN MODEL OF SECULARISM. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Secularism is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that secularismSecularismPolitical Theory 111 Chapter 8 Secularism When different cultures and communities exist within the same country, how should a democratic state ensure equality for each of them?

The text explains that this is the question that emerged in the previous chapter.

The text explains that in this chapter we will try and see how the concept of secularism may be applied to answer that concern.

The text explains that in India, the idea of secularism is ever present in public debates and discussions, yet there is something very perplexing about the state of secularism in India.

The text explains that on the one hand, almost every politician swears by it.

The text explains that every political party professes to be secular.

The text explains that on the other hand, all kinds of anxieties and doubts beset secularism in India.

The text explains that secularism is challenged not only by clerics and religious nationalists but by some politicians, social activists and even academics.

The text explains that in this chapter we will engage in this ongoing debate by asking the following questions: o What is the meaning of secularism? o Is secularism a western implant on Indian soil? o Is it suitable for societies where religion continues to exercise a strong influence on individual lives? o Does secularism show partiality?

The text explains that does it ‘pamper’ minorities? o Is secularism anti-religious?

The text explains that at the end of this chapter you should be able to understand and appreciate the importance of secularism in a democratic society like India, and learn something about the distinctiveness of Indian secularism.

The text explains that secularismSecularism 112 Political Theory 8.1 WHAT IS SECULARISM?

The text explains that though Jews faced discrimination for centuries throughout Europe, in the present state of Israel, A rab minorities, both Christian and Muslims, are excluded from social, political and economic benefits available to Jewish citizens.

The text explains that subtle forms of discrimination also continue to persist against non-Christians in several parts of Europe.

The text explains that the condition of religious minorities in the neighbouring states of Pakistan and Bangladesh has also generated considerable concern.

The text explains that such examples remind us of the continuing importance of secularism for people and societies in today’s world.

The text explains that inter-religious Domination In our own country, the Constitution declares that every Indian citizen has a right to live with freedom and dignity in any part of the country.

The text explains that it is a normative doctrine which seeks to realise a secular society, i.e., one devoid of

The text explains that within this larger framework, let us now consider a narrower and more specific question, namely: What kind of state is necessary to realise these goals?

The text explains that how they function is bound to make a crucial difference to the outcome of any struggle to create a society less ridden with inter-community conflict and religious discrimination.

The text explains that for this reason, we need to see what kind of state is needed to prevent religious conflict and to promote religious harmony.

The text explains that a state governed directly by a priestly order is called theocratic.

The text explains that a secular state must be committed to principles and goals which are at least partly derived from non-religious sources.

The text explains that however, there is no reason to suggest that this separation should take a particular form.

The text explains that there is little scope for community-based rights or minority rights.

The text explains that the history of western societies tells us why this is so.

The text explains that while strict separation of the state from the church is emphasised to realise among other things, individual freedom, issues of inter-religious (and therefore of minority rights) equality are often neglected.

The text explains that this feature follows directly from its understanding that the separation of state from church/ religion entails a relationship of mutual exclusion.

The text explains that but a careful reading of our Constitution shows that this is not the case.

The text explains that nehru himself played a key role in enacting laws abolishing caste discrimination, dowry and sati, and extending legal rights and social freedom to Indian women.

The text explains that this is a great virtue if a society is recovering from a major civil war but not in times of peace where people are struggling for equal dignity and respect.

The text explains that do you remember the heated debate in France over the French government's decision to ban the usage of religious markers like turbans and veils in educational institutions?

The text explains that thus Indian secularism took on a distinct form as a result of an interaction between what already existed in a society that had religious diversity and the ideas that came from the west.

The text explains that secularismSecularismPolitical Theory 119 oppression of dalits and women within Hinduism, the discrimination against women within Indian Islam or Christianity, and the possible threats that a majority community might pose to the rights of the minority religious communities.

The text explains that within it, an individual has the right to profess the religion of his or her choice.

The text explains that likewise, religious minorities also have a right to exist and to maintain their own culture and educational institutions.

The text explains that thus, the Indian constitution bans untouchability.

The text explains that the question however that arises is: can a state initiate or even support religious reforms and yet be secular?

The text explains that thus, the Indian Constitution grants all religious minorities the right to establish and maintain their own educational institutions which may receive assistance from the state.

The text explains that what ideals of secularism do they depict? o Read a short story ‘Name’ in Forsaking Paradise: Stories from Ladakh by Abdul Ghani Sheikh [Published by Katha) LET’S THINK Is secularism compatible with the following? o Subsidising a pilgrimage for a minority community. o Performing religious rituals in government offices.

The text explains that the real question is not whether something is undermined but whether what is undermined is intrinsically worthy or unworthy.

The text explains that the mutual exclusion of religion and state, which is supposed to be the ideal of western secular societies, is also not the defining feature of all secular states.

The text explains that a secular state may keep a principled distance from religion to promote peace between communities and it may also intervene to protect the rights of specific communities.

The text explains that it is true that Indian secularism advocates minority rights so the question is: Is this justified?

The text explains that a person has a prior right to the satisfaction of his or her significant interests.

The text explains that the most fundamental interest of minorities must not be harmed and must be protected by constitutional law.

The text explains that this is exactly how it is in the Indian Constitution.

The text explains that (f) Appointment of temple management bodies by the government.

The text explains that (g) Intervention of state to ensure entry of Dalits in temples.

The text explains that some of the key characteristics of western and Indian model of secularism have got mixed up.

The text explains that secularismSecularism 128 Political Theory Exercises 3.

The text explains that give reasons for supporting or opposing any of them.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 8: Secularism

schemes.name: No
schemes.point: No policy of the state can have an exclusively religious rationale. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch8 pg 5)

schemes.name: No
schemes.point: No religious classification can be the basis of any public policy. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch8 pg 5)

schemes.name: Similarly
schemes.point: Similarly, the state cannot aid any religious institution. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch8 pg 5)

schemes.name: For
schemes.point: For example, if a religious institution forbids a woman from becoming a priest, then the state can do little about it. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch8 pg 5)

schemes.name: On
schemes.point: On this view, religion is a private matter, not a matter of state policy or law. (NCERT Class 11 Political Theory Ch8 pg 5)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 8.1 WHAT IS SECULARISM? and 8.2 SECULAR STATE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Secularism; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Secularism through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 8.1 WHAT IS SECULARISM? and 8.2 SECULAR STATE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
